Osa Conservation: Piro Restoration Experiment Osa Conservation Wildlife Team and Earthwatch Updated on 2026-03-06 1 Introduction The following represents the data exploration for the data collected during the collaboration between Osa Conservation and Earthwatch for the project entitled: A multi-taxa approach comparing the success of active tropical rainforest restoration with old-growth forest in Costa Rica. Also known as: The Piro Restoration Experiment The centralized document for the protocols used in this experiment lives here. Note, as this is a working document only OC staff currently have access. 1.1 Study area and survey locations The following map shows the locations of all the surveys included within this project to date, coloured by simplfied treatment category: active restoration; natural restoration and forest control. The number of survey locations for each category are as follows: Treatment Number of Locations active_restoration 30 forest 24 passive_restoration 10 1.2 Historical survey timeline NOTE We still need incorporate the vegetation data [Also - should we add historical dung beetle and butterfly data?] 1.3 Earthwatch survey timeline Now lets zoom in on data collected since our collaboration with Earthwatch started: And a summary of the number of different surveys conducted by year: Number of unique site-date surveys by type and year Survey Type 2017 2019 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 Total Birds 117 95 105 0 0 0 152 0 469 Bats 0 0 0 200 240 240 239 19 938 Camera traps 0 0 0 0 0 0 155 0 155 Amphibians 120 121 124 56 0 0 68 0 489 Small mammals 0 0 0 0 0 0 19 0 19 Total 237 216 229 256 240 240 633 19 2070

8 Vegetation data

8 Vegetation data To date we have performed surveys (blue dots) in the following 50 locations. We could use grass cover a a proxy for how well the original treatments worked - i.e. if there is very high ground cover than would suggest the trees are not doing particularly well. I have explored the mean grass cover (number of quadrants which contain gress) as the metric for each location: Some sites have low grass cover, but it doesn’t appear to be linked to the original restoration methodology (L,M,H,N). High seems to be the worst, and medium the best! We can do the same but with leaf litter cover (in this instance higher values are better): And shrub cover - note higher values of this are not necessarily good: Canopy cover
summarizing-data-for-next-steps.html
9 Summarizing data for next steps: 9.1 We can see the trends from satellite imagery. 9.2 Lets take a look on how the Biomass spread along our study area 9.3 Final sites selection

9 Summarizing data for next steps: Our monitoring process from the first 8 years of the restoration provide a robust information about the responses of wildlife on the first most-critical moment on recovery. From now on, the surveys can be performed in fewer plots, covering the current trends of recovery. 9.1 We can see the trends from satellite imagery. Based on Planet’s satellite capabilities, we can use biomass and other satellite imagery to monitor restoration recovery by establishing a historical baseline and tracking changes over time. High-frequency, daily imagery from satellites allows for the continuous observation of vegetation regrowth of specific recovery areas, providing quantifiable evidence of restoration success. For example, if we compare the Biomass (Mg/ha) per site through time, we can clearly see its increase since 2017, right when the restoration began. However, it is still lower than the biomass in pristine forest so the restoration is still on-course. Lets make a close-up to the restoration sites. See that the trends are not related to our initial classification H-M-L-N. So the response per each site is very uneven. Those are the expected responses to restoration, the heterogenity on soil, flooding, among others makes the tree growing varies in space. 9.2 Lets take a look on how the Biomass spread along our study area 9.3 Final sites selection Once the current trends were well established, we can reduce the number of restoration plots to be assessed from now on. Whit this selection, we can perform more effective sampling surveys for the ongoing years. But, what plots should be keep monitoring? To do so, we checked first the survey locations of bats, amphibians and birds. As you can see, not all surveys were done always in the same locations, so the selected plots must cover the entire recovery and sampling scenarios. Here you can compare the Biomass trends between the overall and the selected plots, on Biomass for 2024 and Biomass change between 2017 and 2024. Lets see the location of the selected plots. Now, we are ready for our ongoing surveys!
